mission.title: Install and Configure Telepresence on Kubernetes
mission.description: Telepresence is a tool that allows you to connect your local workstation to a remote Kubernetes or OpenShift cluster, enabling you to run services locally while accessing cluster resources. This setup facilitates fast local development without the need for lengthy container build and deployment cycles.

steps.title: Add the Telepresence Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Telepresence Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration. This repository contains the necessary charts for deploying Telepresence components.
```bash
helm repo add telepresence-oss https://getambassador.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Telepresence Traffic Manager
steps.description:
Deploy the Telepresence Traffic Manager to your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. Make sure to specify the namespace where you want to install it.
```bash
helm install telepresence-traffic-manager telepresence-oss/telepresence-oss --namespace telepresence --create-namespace --version 1.1.1-bogus.overwritten.by.chart.go
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Telepresence Traffic Manager pod is running successfully. This step ensures that the installation was successful and that the service is operational.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace telepresence
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Set resource limits for the Telepresence Traffic Manager to ensure it runs efficiently within your cluster. Edit the values.yaml file or use `--set` flags to specify resource limits during installation.
```bash
helm upgrade telepresence-traffic-manager telepresence-oss/telepresence-oss --namespace telepresence --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=256Mi
```

steps.title: Access the Telepresence Traffic Manager
steps.description:
You can port-forward the Traffic Manager service to access it locally. This allows you to interact with the service from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/telepresence-traffic-manager --namespace telepresence 8081:8081
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Telepresence will result in the Traffic Manager pod running in the specified namespace, and you will be able to access it via port forwarding. You can now start developing locally while interacting with your remote Kubernetes cluster.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add telepresence-oss https://getambassador.io
helm repo update
helm upgrade telepresence-traffic-manager telepresence-oss/telepresence-oss --namespace telepresence --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=256Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Telepresence Traffic Manager release from your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall telepresence-traffic-manager --namespace telepresence
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that may have been created during the installation. This ensures that all resources are properly cleaned up.
```bash
kubectl delete crd telepresence.*
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace telepresence
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Telepresence namespace to remove any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been deleted successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace telepresence
kubectl get namespaces | grep telepresence
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Telepresence Traffic Manager. This can be done by exporting the current Helm values.
```bash
helm get values telepresence-traffic-manager --namespace telepresence > telepresence-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade the Telepresence Traffic Manager to the latest version. Replace '1.1.2' with the desired version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade telepresence-traffic-manager telepresence-oss/telepresence-oss --namespace telepresence --version 1.1.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Telepresence Traffic Manager pod is running the new version and is operational. This ensures that the upgrade was successful.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace telepresence
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Telepresence Traffic Manager pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors. This can indicate issues with the configuration or resource limits.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace telepresence
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Telepresence Traffic Manager service, ensure that the port-forwarding command is running and check the service status. Verify that the correct ports are being forwarded.
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace telepresence
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, check the currently installed version and ensure that the version you are trying to upgrade to is compatible. You can view the installed version with:
```bash
helm list --namespace telepresence
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources error
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an insufficient resources error, it may indicate that the resource limits set for the Traffic Manager are too high for your cluster. Adjust the resource limits and try again.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace telepresence
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
